poems.scratchpad:
just as we really get going
the big hill stands in our way

five speed drop handled racer

the straight uphill
when it slows you
shows what a hero can do

struggle up
don't give up
greet the brow at the top
greet the view

on we go
between the hawthorn hedges
following the tangled edges
of each field

a big cow clap
causes swerves and curses
watch out for that
the leader urges
as we make our way

struggle up the other side to gain
a tight thin road that wanders
the boundary of every field
hid behind big hedgerows
waiting for the rain

but not today
there's not a cloud in the sky
we're going on our way
we're on a cycle ride
summer's day
we're on a cycle ride
pedal away
we're on a cycle ride
what can i say
we're on a cycle ride

tree trunks are baggy old camoflage pants
snagged on rusty bits of barbed wire
private wood pigeon is off duty snoring
there's gunpowder grit in my tyre

major five bar galvanised gate
commands his regiment of sentry cement poles
seargent jackdaw's goose step gait
struts the cattle field looking for worm holes

five speed drop handled racer
it's a new bike i got it this year
freewheeling momentum chaser
watch out the big hill is here

two wheel momentum chaser

days of waterlogged wellies
searching for the source of the Nile

wee brother Tim is a racer
ram stam forging ahead

i'm the laid back day dreamer
freewheeling thoughts in my head

behind lags Barry the fatty
wheezy freckled red head

empty road
single file

silly catch phrases
scraps of pop songs
poems.text:
between the lawnmowers and the deep-freeze
I unhook my bike and bounce it off the concrete floor
we're going on a cycle ride
let's take the Quaterlands Road to the towpath

stand up on each pedal
ring ring ring the bell
gravel rattles mudguards to the gate
two gardeners from the long grass call
look out for cars and don't be late

race you to the crossroads
into Mrs Pedlow's shop
change pocket money into liquorice
we're on our way
it's a sunny day
the straps on our saddlebags flap

the Hillhall bus is at the bus stop
it must be three o'clock
if we're going to go where we want to go
we'd better hurry up

the long slow curve of the main road
cuts flat the countryside
after finishing off the sherbet
we put down our heads and ride

hug the kerb
hold steady for lorries
count telephone poles as they pass
school uniform lurks in the cupboard
September is lost in the past

and then we turn off
down the Quaterlands
pedal like mad and glide
whizz over the shuck at the bottom
glimpse the swamp where we used to hide

suddenly next it's the big hill
the one we can never get up
wiggle and wobble and waggle
waggle and wobble and stop
poems.title: Cycle ride

Christopher's gone loopy

surprised that I am sane
I'll sort it out don't worry

out the gate
down the road
novice priest that night

under an unreachable sky

into the Homestead Inn

gloomy pub world seats
staring into his grievances
with girlfriend along side

what is this all about
everyone's to blame
they're just our poor parents
imperfect we're all the same

poems.text:
rattling down the hill
tried to limbo under
ragged wire fence

find a long bad gash 
left in my upper arm
poems.title: Racing driver

poems.text:
red bucket beneath the sink
full of potato peelings apple cores 
banana skins carrot tops 
courgettes ends 
onion skins

go put the rubbish on the compost heap

run like greased lightning
duck dodging the branch
watch that slippery patch
where the sewage tank
overflow seeps

down the paving stone
path between pea rows
Jerusalem artichokes and
the glass frames to protect
the lettuces from the frost

warm smell grass cutting mound
beside pile up branches and sticks

up end the bucket
bang out the sticky bits
high tail it back to the house

wave empty bucket in
rattling swinging arcs
poems.title: Red bucket

poems.text:
semi-accidental group of youth
hunkered shelter chat

rhododendron leaves
fringe of drips
motley band of us
waiting out the rain

what's that you're reading
some old chinese joke book
tall Mary with the thin fair hair
didn't really catch my meaning

small black line
danced before our eyes

I asked 
they all said 
they could see it
poems.title: Tao Te Ching

poems.text:
rolled up copies of Fiesta
damp in a plastic bag
pulled out of hedge

further up where they 
straightened the road
old bend half hidden
neither road nor lane
just left abandoned

forgotten corner where 
I hide feast my eyes on
tanned tones and curves

got it out under my coat
poems.title: Afternoon bike ride

poems.text:
round the garden roams
a wooly hat and cardigan
following the progress of
every single plant
poems.title: Gardener

poems.text:
so this is where he lives
short row of terrace houses
other side of the Lagan
up near the spinning mill

come over to my place
I want to play you something

record player in his bedroom

listen to this
did you hear it
he's singing milly

sounds like immediately to me

no it's not
I'm telling you
he's from Belfast

diamond stylus
fine groove vinyl
don't scratch it
poems.title: Rufus McFadden

poems.scratchpad:
tell me Michael
have you got a girlfriend yet

chickens and tomatoes
i like Ethel
she greets me cheerfully
and with respect
casually chatting
like an active version of Granny Rip

let's me play with the egg grader

how come her kitchen's like this
enormous old porcelain sink
pipe on the wall not in it
dark small window no view

come in and sit down and have a biscuit
weird to be sitting looking at the front lawn
biscuit tin on the table in front of me
just you sit there a minute
poems.text:
between high banks of tough grass
pushing the big corrugated gate

empty outhouses
smelling of straw

admire the logical
egg sorting machine

front door lawn drive
deep hidden in trees

the real front door is the back door
through the pantry into the kitchen

everyone's children are chickens
poems.title: Run up to Ethel's and get some eggs

poems.text:
down a side street
across a car park
it's all normal

long dull building
tall thin silent ex-Inst boy
doing A Levels again

Bay City Rollers 
two haircut brothers
friendly married girl
poems.title: Rupert Stanley College of Higher Education

poems.text:
sandy specked jelly
poked with a stick
already dead

armless menace
sting coloured pink
drift in waist high waves
poems.title: Jellyfish

poems.text:
seaside
run and hide

rock pool
water's cool

starfish
make a wish

seaweed 
book to read

big shell
listen well

cold wave
stone to save

sunbeam
dam the stream

driftwood
time for food

picnic
play a trick

sand dune
sing a tune

dry bone
driving home
poems.title: Connemara

poems.text:
send me 
the Little Red Schoolbook

streets of London
are paved with revolution
l'm going to come to London

didn't get 
a Little Red Schoolbook

streets of London 
don't care for revolution
no don't come to London
poems.title: Little Red Schoolbook

poems.scratchpad:
she must have had her eye on me
because when i'd finished what i said
she spoke up from the other end of the row
where we sat waiting for the lecturer to come
you only say things like that to be different
poems.text:
when I tell her I'm tripping
she stands childlike on stage
asking do I look like a dragon
poems.title: Normal girl

poems.scratchpad:
sitting in Ormeau Park near the Ulster Museum
Foundation course is over
my head's shorn
got all my stuff in a kit bag
i'm gone
moody
and wondering
meanwhile two toughs
line up with the ends of my bench
like two sentries
from the corner of my eye
i see a belt slowly unbuckling
what are you doing here
i'm waiting for the bus
they obviously think i'm some kind of terrorist
prove it
my name is Scott
so therefore my family
dates back to the first
Scotish Protestants
luckily they didn't ask
for the fifth verse
of the national anthem

sat in the park
between museum and bus
like a Van Gogh
that Roderick O'Connor

surly denim sentinels
sullen each end of my bench
danger alert
oh shit
corner of eye fingers
unbuckle
draw out
coil around knuckle
leather belt
poems.text:
damp neat municipal park
kill time before home on the bus

that Roderick O'Connor
is just like a Van Gogh

pair of denim sentinels
somehow stood sullen 
each end of my bench

oh shit

out of the corner of my eye
fingers unbuckle draw out
coil around knuckles
a thick leather belt
poems.title: Prods

poems.text:
slip skip down the path
skidding on dusty heels

slip skip almost broke
She Loves You by the Beatles
poems.title: Leaving school

poems.text:
soft and late
curled in pyjamas

on the sofa
they're walking on the moon
poems.title: History

poems.text:
another car flashes its lights
he doesn't wait in the queue

turns off down Balmoral
let's try find another way

Lisburn Road's no better
flackjacket waving us on

mirrors and windscreen wipers
putting the cheques in the bank
poems.title: Don’t get stuck

poems.text:
something's happened to Grandpa Rip
Mummy Daddy gone to take care of it

Uncle Barry here in his pyjamas
I'm sleeping on the floor

he's standing above me
trying to get us to sleep

I'm looking at his toe

big colossal enormously huge
twisted encrusted like a shipwreck
poems.title: Shipwreck

poems.text:
speedreader
ordered through the post
brown plastic
shutter window flashing
lines of text

skim the line
read faster

never worked

words are mulled over
communed with
enjoyed

GBS prefaces at lunchtime
top floor
English block
by the window
at the radiator
looking down on the quad
poems.title: Device

poems.scratchpad:
used to be bad in the 20s
some ancient sectarian divide
maybe we cut short our holidays
radio shows people burnt out of their homes

headlands ocean and sand dunes
wee caravan light in the night
i caught an enormous pollock
there are hippie beads under my shirt
poems.text:
I'm stood in a farm in Belmullet
watching a lobster boil in a pot

later I hear Mum Dad talking
about some troubles 
that've started 
again
poems.title: Belmullet

poems.text:
stop
get out
close the gate

one side then the other

the latch 
the drop bolt
the Longshot discus thrower

wanted a simple country gate
painted deep cadmium yellow

up there the branch broke

stunned to have grit in my forehead
ran the long gravel drive blubbering
poems.title: Close the gate

poems.text:
straggle gaggle all day hike
tiny giants stride the Isle of Man

rucksacks treetrunk unpacked lunch
someone's tuned their transistor
BBC seriously says the news

homos over 21
something between men is now legal

pack up
maps out
head for the ridge
tag beside our careful leader

embark on a tentative meeting of minds
poems.title: Homosexual

poems.text:
stroking Snowy's purry fur
scratch at Snowy's gooey chin

lie with Snowy chewing grass
I spy Snowy climb a tree

shut up Snowy you've just been fed
watch out Snowy's tail is twitching

Snowy gobs up a ball of fur
Snowy killed by a car

Snowy Two is just like Snowy One
except for the bad ear that got cut off
eventually we had to put her down

Tio Pepe is like marmalade
Daddy called him after the sherry
when he started squirting all over
Mummy had his balls cut off
poems.title: Snowy

poems.text:
light through the window
dark empty room

write it out
one hundred times

I before E
except after C

my exception proves their rule
poems.title: T H E I R

poems.text:
clean off bark
carve a man's head

decapitate fields of grass
crumple poor cow parsley
poems.title: Sword stick

poems.text:
tall deep narrow street Queens Street
dull flat and closed door
Art School Foundation Year
did Latin instead of Art
no portfolio
Mum tries to solve
the problem of what
her Michael should do
now that he has failed
so many exams

small slim brown and black portfolio
labelled left against the pile
in the corner in the corridor

rejected without an interview
poems.title: Portfolio

poems.scratchpad:
she's tall and she's pretty
married as a sister would be

and the pavement joins us in two
poems.text:
tall patient quiet
long dark hair
married

walking side by side
patient with me talking
pavement joins us into two

Rupert Stanley to the junction 
of the Malone and Lisburn Road 

two tall gentle walkers before parting
with what was never said or was it

gave it that almost special flavour
poems.title: Companion

poems.text:
the backdoor keys
old wheelless trolley
Pepe curl up basket

boiler room that once
contained dirty coal

grimy old coal man long ago
poems.title: Backdoor

poems.text:
old caravan door
don't close well
good slam shut

gaslight mantle
splutter flares
gas goes out
poems.title: Caravan

poems.text:
living room feels
like a photograph

the cowrie shell
the little glass birds
the bookshelves
the wallpaper
the strip of wood around the fireplace
the brown tweed on the sofa
the little green swivel chair

the old kind of brown
plastic castors that
protect the parquet from
the legs of the chairs
that sometimes press into
the carpet

the dried flowers honesty
corn heads grasses
the box with the coal bucket

Mum's chest with its layers
and layers of folded fabric
sewing stuff knitting needles
wool patterns

the light that swings from the wall

the green glass sphere
on the marble bowl
on the high windowsill
at the end of the dining table
the lampshade that they made
with the funny bread loaf
weight for raising it when
there were parties

the box of inherited cutlery
under the side board
poems.title: Livingroom

poems.scratchpad: Ballyaughlis crossroads
poems.text:
flat grey gable of the Homestead
bottom of the Drumbo Road

old boulder built into the corner of it
echos solid trunks 
grass bank wire and field

skip sprint keep eye out for cars
the camber the texture the tarmac
fine gravel along the edge of road

foot after foot
laboured pace or funny walk
swinging leather satchel from straps

there's less traffic on the Drumbo Road

walking up between steep grass banks
gashed earth where some mad geezer
must've had to swerve

Gauguin in Brittany
painted hedgerows like that
tangle bramble tweedy textured clumps

and down there 
Eddy Bell's vegetable garden all laid out
i've painted some cottage pictures
always seen from certain angles
never entered

remember the side field flooded
that was great
skimming the ice on the ice

and now on this final flat bit
our buttercup yellow gate appears

foot after heel to toe foot
dawdling
poems.title: Drumbo Road

poems.text:
famous
signposted
empty

dolmen
caveman's kennel
cemented
littered

the ring mound
round
uneventful

couple of visits
enough

more potent
lures the towpath
the Lagan

empty windows of linen history
poems.title: The Giant’s Ring

poems.text:
the waterfall i painted
with me in front
with Gauguin Breton colours
and lines round things
is where we played
and dammed
and cleared a whole new water course
that's it there on the lefthand side
that waterfall was important then
poems.title: Waterfall

poems.text:
Sunday afternoon
let's go for a drive

window handle
the winderuper
it's a little loose
fingers wobble it

nose and cheek
against the glass
not really interested
in farms and hedges
poems.title: Germ of elsewhere forming

poems.text:
there'll be no one home on Saturday
have the whole house to myself

they'll be home any minute now
sound of tyres on gravel
poems.title: No one home

poems.text:
there's a tiny bottle of beer
in there in the family museum

eventually I drank it

a spiky dry lizard
shells pebbles and rocks
and a lock of Daddy's baby hair

memorial coins
glass sliding doors
shut and rattle a little

the big seed from Jamacia
that we found on the beach

wild Atlantic

Grandpa's tastled German dagger
poems.title: Wee gottle of geer

poems.text:
they live in a valley
the river is raging
the image is grainy
struggling world
far away
poems.title: Documentary

poems.scratchpad:
this room is our room
we call it the playroom
the room where we spend so much time

four Harlequin doors
wings of the table
mouse tail plant
poems.text:
gaze out playroom window
we always called it that

cold mug on the radiator
TV with Pepe the cat

abstract bird table sculpture
made because the other one died

stand here think of the future
watching life paths divide
poems.title: Waiting for replies

poems.text:
tie-fly fingers
Boy Scout thumb

here it is
my little silver chillum

scrap of tinfoil
wrapped around a Biro

took one puff and
where the hell did i go
poems.title: Tinfoil

poems.text:
timetable says
after break

Mr Longley
English Lit

Scott shut up
for goodness sake

shut up yourself
you gloomy git
poems.title: Timetable

poems.text:
Timothy's on up ahead
Barry as usual is last

i'm the one in the middle
neither too slow nor too fast
poems.title: Cycling

poems.scratchpad:
the Tom Caldwell Gallery
has an opening tonight
should i wear my pale blue polo neck

a room full of people
glasses of wine
should i wear my psychedelic tie

if you know someone

you stand about talking

i'm dressed up embarrassed and shy

i pick up an underage glass
and sip it while looking at the pictures

small frames hanging
from aluminium rods
with labels pinned on white hessian

there's Solly Lipshitz
and a fat grey hair lady
they seem to be drinking a lot

Mum and Dad
talk to Betty and Bruce
Arthur Bookman and Erna

the one i liked
was an odd long legged crow
Daddy bought a surrealist invention

some are proper
dressed in suits
a few more casual and sloppy

an awkward crow in a field
a silly surrealist invention
poems.text:
Tom Caldwell Gallery
has an opening tonight
I love my pale blue polo

a room full of people
getting glasses of wine
I love my psychedelic tie

if you know someone
you stand around talking

if like me you don't
you go around the wall
poems.title: Art event

poems.scratchpad:
worry's white hair makes him younger

he's not really my uncle just Barry's dad
always ready for questions and banter
we challenge each other's opinions
poems.text:
tootling past stone walls 
out of Killybegs town
Uncle Bonar demos his 
double declutch

cruise into the curve
did you hear the wheels squeek
sure old Jemimah 
she's as good as 
an MG
poems.title: Double declutch

poems.text:
I'm trying to paint 
a Basil Blackshaw 
hill full of silage

solitary hawthorn 
against silver sky

over the hill comes a tractor
methodically cutting grass 
right before my very eye
poems.title: Basil Blackshaw

poems.text:
peat streams clean gravel
rocky pools cut the bog

fly tie wanderer
rod in hand
lands a little fish

dumb gasp
gawps at me

wet hand quivers
unhooking sadness
poems.title: Peat streams

poems.text:
two beautiful boughs
twin columns of gold

instincts arouse
feelings unfold

Ashleigh girls
wear the greenest green
that's the shortest skirt 
I've ever seen

Kate Wilde skips off 
the 22 bus 
at Ballylesson
poems.title: Kate Wilde

poems.text:
two tackle bags 
in the middle of the boat
flylines curl out in the night

careful with that oar 
or you'll scare off the fish

catching's just an excuse to fishing
poems.title: Fly fishing

poems.text:
up each nostril
digging for snot

hoiking it out to
see what we got

juicy big oyster or
shiny wee pearl

it's a wonder
all the pleasure
we can find in this world
poems.title: Nose picker

poems.scratchpad:
big room
bright room
rug world
Shykel Mott
smashing clockwork train
it's raining wooden blocks
poems.text:
used so much dynamite
had to make it in black and white
stopping the Nazi train

French Old Masters packed in crates
down the line the ambush waits
stopping the Nazi train
poems.title: The Train

poems.text:
in Granny Scott's
big maroon armchair
I'm watching BBC

Modigliani velvet suit
leaps on a gravestone
spouts an insatiable Art

living room bookcase
Phaidon high up books
investigate all afternoon

Central Library
Dewey 920 bio
no need school 

thumb in palette
mix the colours
turpentine smell

what to paint
my imagination
house and trees
poems.title: Bohemia

poems.text:
watch out for guppy spikes 
in wet sand jab your foot
nasty legless dragons
poems.title: Guppy spikes

poems.text:
we thought i was getting a cold
but we went all the same
out in the boat to the island
in vast inaccessible Lough Neagh
you can't even see it from the shore

the Bulls were looking after it
for the National Trust
a low dark damp cottage
with a big map of the island
painted on the wall

right out into the jungle
running paths
with sticks in our hands
this way that way
and then abruptly a hole
full of grey water
with a brown hairy ominous back
floating drowned dead
spirit of the dead dead
donkey
we stopped
hovering
recoiling from an accident
that perhaps required adult intervention

back squealing recovering fun
poems.title: Lough Neigh

poems.text:
we Ulstermen 
must be Ulstermen
big teeth chin and nose
poems.title: Ulstermen

poems.text:
we've got a swing in our garden
we can swing on it 
seven days a week

of course we don't use it that often
but when we do
it never fails to squeak

sometimes I look up at Orion
and from the Plough 
pick out the North Pole star

we're on the ground 
but through the sky 
we're flying

who knows what 
or where or why 
we really are
poems.title: Swing

poems.text:
weird feeling inside easy to hide
dashboard steering wheel Daddy
tip of my finger touching my thumb
shout whisper feather weight numb
poems.title: Weird

poems.text:
what's he doing
he should be studying

making my Romeo shirt
floppy sleeves no collar
ties at neck and wrists

pale creamy yellow
fabric a bit too heavy
never wore it really

what's he doing
he should be studying

guilty resentful feeling
Daddy's right

mother and son playing 
primary school dressmaking
make believe lanky troubadour

what's he doing
he should be studying
poems.title: Tension

poems.text:
when Grandpa Rip
planned this house
he made a big living room

parquet floors
french windows
fireplace
but it's cold

and Mummy likes to keep it clean

so we sit in the playroom
at the front
radiator television
fold away table
every night the BBC news

it freaks me out
to see us all like zombies
glued to the goggle box

in the living room
on the sofa

on Granny Scott's fox furs
that Timothy sewed together

i'm reading
Shamanism by
Mircea Eliade
poems.title: Unused room

poems.text:
wander through 
these back streets
really lose my way
think about my father
in the office in the factory

this city 
its industry is grim
it would be a sin for me to stay here

must get out
the way my father stayed
was not to be an artist
and get out

where am I
little houses
all the same
who lives in them
the working class

Protestant 
or Catholic
what area am I in

better get out
up to the Lisburn Road
where it's safer
I can get a bus
poems.title: Back streets

poems.text:
with closed hand
against his mouth

burp

Dad gets up
goes into the kitchen

teaspoon handle under lid
bright yellow tin of BiSoDol
poems.title: Burp

poems.text:
yes yes
no no
off to bed we go

but
no buts
no
off to bed you go

i know
i know
off to bed i go
poems.title: Bedtime

poems.scratchpad:
chose tubes random
squeeze blobs down edge
scrape steady ruler
wasteland drama space

snip round
Sunday Times colour supplement
GI gun helmet Yanks
carefully paste them down
war hell men nowhere
we are public political pop
poems.text:
choose tubes 
at random

squeeze blobs
steady scrape with a ruler

make a wasteland drama space

snip around 
American GIs 
from the Sunday supplement

carefully place them down

this is war hell men

a nowhere 
public political pop
poems.title: Pop Art

poems.text:
the mother's hand
that holds the bowl
gives blackberry 
and apple
poems.title: What's for pudding

poems.text:
plastic picnic mug
lawnmower driving
fallow
silage
how to peel an apple
how to peel an orange
poems.title: Mother

poems.scratchpad:
pulling out a big fly leg

sideways on
from below
see the silver glasses twinkle

thin silvery specs
oldlike
rocklike
well worn wood

white bristles

father and son
the tradition of the Scott moustache

Daddy's is wider
Grandpa's is whiter

no naked upper lip

white white bristles
join a strong sturdy nose

did a little hand
reach out to clutch them
those shiny bright white bristles

bushy upper lip
bristly white white bristles

bushy white white bristle mouth
nostril sturdy nose
grey white
white white
brushed with the back of a finger

bushy white bristle mouth
meets nostrilly nose

bushy bright white bristle mouth

wide white soft white bristles

fly leg lair full of hair
Daddy's got them too
poems.text:
bushy bright white bristle mouth
meets nostrilly nostrilly nose
poems.title: The Scott tradition

poems.text:
climb up sit

and bump
and bump
and whoopsy daisy

laughter peeling off my face
deep incapacitating giggles

again do it 
again Grandpa
poems.title: Big cavalry twill knees

poems.text:
Mum near the kitchen
Dad at the head
against the wall

looking down the table
and the room
boys next to Mum
I bags the end
apex
side for one
on my left
Granny and Grandpa Scott

later
on my left
Grandpa Scott

that's Daddy's Dad
poems.title: Sunday lunchtime table

poems.text:
old lonely man
can't see inside

so solid
so far away
poems.title: Lonely grandpa

poems.text:
where did Daddy get his artistic
how did I grow up in an arty home
how did Daddy get to be artistic

Granny bossy fussy
everyone at the table
ready right on time

cheerful 
buck toothed
three sister smile

memories of pleasure 
her warbly voice which
momentarily fills the car
musical but not artistic

Grandpa is a grown old
handsome practical man
settled for mystery Shell 
BP office job long ago

an understanding of 
interlocking parts
but not artistic
poems.title: Artistic

poems.text:
grease dirt grit
snake bicycle chain
slide the clip unloop it

three speed six speed
traveling gears wire 
pull leaver heave
poems.title: Bicycle chain

poems.text:
Christopher has some pills
they gave him because
all the stress of studying

pills
drugs
Valium
Michael

Mum shouts Michael
wakes me for lunch
I must have crashed out

oh shit
sitting by the pond
under the hessian canopy
on top of the clothes line

god I'm dozy
they're suspicious
what's wrong with you

my opaque secret life
tune into the outside world
experiment with drugs
far out

zombie eats his salad
poems.title: Valium

poems.scratchpad:
Ulster fry happiness
The Generation Game
poems.text:
it's hard to get close to Dad
in his suit and off to work
buried in the factory

at weekends
Saturday at least
he likes to get into the garden

gardening clothes
old trousers welly boots
the ragged stag and fir tree cardigan
the little milky coffee woolly hat

coming in after dark
winter earth cold opened
hacks across his knuckles
needing New Skin later

mug of tea
Ten O'Clock News
late night cigarette
poems.title: Close to Dad

poems.text:
I like the way he soaks
sheets of Arches in the bath

I like the way he let his hair grow
after fights about the length of mine

brushes are fussy
palette knife is free

sinking under the 6 o'clock
the 9 o'clock 
the 10 o'clock news

it's nice when he smiles
especially now with the missing tooth
poems.title: Daddy’s reprieve

poems.text:
empty house
arouse
empty house
secrets
private life

naked
at the bottom of the stairs
tall enough
in the hall mirror
in my mother's bra and tights
how much further can i go
poems.title: Transgression

poems.text:
where did my father get his artistic
once only once
he confided in me
I used to be interested in that
did he have Gauguin dreams too
poems.title: Once

poems.text:
have to go to school
have to go to school

middle aged
adults owners
make all the rules

I hate to go to school
hate to wear a uniform
hate to study do exams

wish I could be me
could be free
poems.title: Prison

poems.text:
somewhere a gun 
to scare off the birds
poems.title: Empty field

poems.text:
cheery bye
don’t bat an eye
go on get out of here
poems.title: How I say it

poems.text:
god Granny's skinny

door open glimpsed
silky dressing gown

deep broken chest
just skin and bone

looks at me calm
helpless revealed

sorry
Granny

I close the door
poems.title: Auschwitz

poems.text:
outside Sligo
near Isle Innisfree

on the rim of a perfect curving amphitheatre field
with the mound of gorse that hides the long lost rath of static time
when there was no draw of city dropout turn on Little Red Schoolbook
Hendrix tie dye psycadelic pot shaped Pink Floyd words
or Janis Joplin's hellfire wild and frantic dazzling hippy festivals
in mud that makes bright faces laugh through tangled hair
night settles us to sit here side by side the space for
two others left between us two Michaels pals at school confederate
ears bound by some knowledge of each other's thoughts

gazing at a lone complete and silent chestnut tree
that stands rooted where it seeded half way up the slope
i raphsodise of some way out of here six form's gone
what's done is done ignorance sedated panic
laughs to note I barely studied lay all summer in the sun
opened books face down on Granny Scott's comfortable
old brown tartan picnic blanket
old cars jaloppy sing song big flasks of tea the freedom of the car
there must be somekind of way out of here
said the joker to the theif
yeah I mean London's the place to go
why hang around here with the Troubles
it's never going to get any better they love it
it'll take an age to change I can see it in my father's face
when he drives to work stuck in those checkpoint queues
we've got to just get up and go
hitch a ride we'll see when we get there
squatters groups and art labs think
poems.text:
on the rim of a perfect 
amphitheatre field with 
a mound of gorse 
behind our backs
and night settling us 

side by side
two Michaels 
pals at school 
share their ears for 
each other's thoughts

gazing at the lone tree 
rooted half way up the slope
I raphsodise of way out of here 

this Ulster dump
where day is normal 
unto day of trouble 

our Troubles
reported on the TV 
tea time local news

what future here I say 
because it seems so clear
that somehow there must be 
some way out of here

sudden and 
I detect embarrassed 
that I race on so far
for his tentative untrained daydreamerless melancholy

he adjusts his fringe parting it 
carefully with two hands
and quietly replies you will go 
you have it in you to leave
but I am going to be stuck here
what does it take to say that 
at this fresh forming age

remember when we would stand 
at the window on the top floor
of the English department 
outside Sleepy Sam's dusty
map of the British Empire room

we used to gaze out of the window 
how old that building was
and talk of how 
it all 
was wrong 
but I'm thinking
all we really talked about was love 
his love

the mutton chopped
romantic lover 
Lenoard Cohen 
moody swamped
in the lightweight troubles of 
what she thinks 
and what she does
and how etcetera 

loyal as a dog I listen and advise
perhaps she etcetera yes 
and so on as our conversation
separated us into the realist 
and the untried big talk fool

the night grew perhaps 
a little chiller 
and it was time
to return to the farmhouse 

Famous Blue Raincoat
sincierely L Cohen
